title: XPS and SEM characterization of zirconia thin films prepared by electrochemical deposition
abstract:
SURFACE AND INTERFACE ANALYSIS
Surf. Interface Anal. 30, 628–631 (2000)
XPS and SEM characterization of zirconia thin
ﬁlms prepared by electrochemical deposition
P. Stefanov,1 D. Stoychev,2 M. Stoycheva,2 J. Ikonomov1 and Ts. Marinova1*
1 Institute of General and Inorganic Chemistry, Bulgarian Academy of Sciences, Soﬁa 1113, Bulgaria
2 Institute of Physical Chemistry, Bulgarian Academy of Sciences, Soﬁa 1113, Bulgaria
Electrochemical deposition of zirconia on stainless steel has been investigated in order to provide supports
of catalysts for NOx reduction. The zirconia ﬁlms have been obtained in electrolytes based on anhydrous
ethyl alcohol. The structure and chemical composition of the ﬁlms have been characterized by SEM and XPS
depth proﬁling. With rising cathodic voltage, the structure and morphology exhibit essential changes. The
most porous structure corresponds to ﬁlms formed at 21 V, whereas the highest density is achieved at 25 V.
The XPS depth proﬁling data show that the composition in the bulk of the ﬁlms is close to stoichiometric.
The cross-section of an annealed ﬁlm gives evidence for good adhesion to the substrate. The ﬁlm thickness
is 3–10 µm. After annealing in air at 550 °C for 1 h, no cracks and inhomogeneities are observed. Copyright
2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEYWORDS: XPS; SEM; stainless steel; electrodeposition; zirconia
INTRODUCTION
Zirconia-based materials are known to have excellent
technological properties such as chemical inertness, ther-
mal stability, mechanical strength and wear resistance.
These properties enable zirconia ﬁlms to be used as cat-
alysts or catalyst supports1–4 in exhaust-gas purifying
devices where high thermal, mechanical and corrosion sta-
bility is required.
The aim of the work reported in this paper is to exam-
ine the structure and the composition of zirconia ﬁlms
deposited electrochemically on stainless steel under differ-
ent regimes. It has been shown that if a suitable deposition
regime is chosen by varying the cathode potential, the cur-
rent density, the time of electrolysis etc., it is possible to
obtain thick ﬁlms having good adhesion to the substrate.
The catalytic application of zirconia ﬁlms requires control-
ling the pore structure, which depends on the microstruc-
ture of the composite particles and their agglomeration
behaviour.5 In addition, knowledge of their surface struc-
ture and chemistry is needed with a view to successive
deposition of transition metal (Co, Cu, Fe, etc.)-based cat-
alysts.
EXPERIMENTAL
Samples of conventional stainless steel 316 L were used.
According to DIN 17440, this steel has the following
* Correspondence to: Ts. Marinova, Institute of General and Inor-
ganic Chemistry, Bulgarian Academy of Sciences, Soﬁa 1113, Bulgaria.
E-mail: marinova@ipchp.ipc.bas.bg
Contract/grant sponsor: EC; Contract/grant number: ENVA-CT97-
0633.
Contract/grant sponsor: National Scientiﬁc Council of Bulgaria; Con-
tract/grant number: X-828.
composition (wt.%): Fe (bal.), Cr (18.00), Ni (12.00), Mn
(<2.00), Mo (2.50), Si (<1.00), C (<0.030), S (<0.045)
and P (<0.03). Substrates with an area of 1 ð 1 cm
and a thickness of 0.5 mm, cut from a standard sheet
of stainless steel, were used for the experiments. Prior to
the electrochemical formation of the oxide layers, stan-
dard procedures of degreasing and pickling were per-
formed.
Zirconia ﬁlms were obtained in a non-aqueous elec-
trolyte based on absolute alcohol with a Zr4C concentration
of 25 g l�1. The anode was insoluble and was made of
platinum sheets (130ð10ð0.5 mm). It had the shape of a
ring, 55 mm in diameter, and the cathode was placed in its
centre. The volume of the glass cell where the electroly-
sis proceeded was 100 ml. In some experiments where
deposition of a more homogeneous layer was needed,
the substrates (cathodes) of stainless steel 316 L were
screened by a frame made of a platinum or a steel ﬁbre
(0.3 mm in diameter) placed at a distance of 50 mm from
the sample periphery. The voltage at which the samples
were deposited ranged from 9 to 35 V. The electrolyte
temperature was 25 °C and the time of electrolysis varied
within the range 1–60 min.
The ﬁlms obtained were investigated by SEM and XPS
in order to characterize the microstructure and chem-
ical composition of the zirconia layers. The electron
microscopy studies were carried out with a JEM-200CX
electron microscope. The XPS studies were performed
in an Escalab II system using Mg K˛ radiation with
an energy of 1253.6 eV. The photoelectron spectra were
quantiﬁed using Scoﬁeld’s photoionization cross-sections6
and the attenuation lengths estimated on the basis of
Ref. 7. The depth proﬁles were obtained with an ion gun
operated at 3 keV (45° incidence angle, 16.0 µA cm�2
ion current density). The sputter rate (3 nm min�1) was
related to that measured using a thin calibrated ZrO2
ﬁlm.8
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 15 July 1999
Revised 24 November 1999; Accepted 24 November 1999
 ZIRCONIA THIN FILMS PREPARED BY ELECTROCHEMICAL DEPOSITION
629
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Film deposition
Two possibilities of electrochemical deposition of zirconia
on a steel surface have been studied: deposition in aqueous
and non-aqueous electrolytes of various zirconia salts
(ZrCl4, Zr(SO4◁2, ZrCl2).
Deposition of zirconia in aqueous electrolytes does not
provide sufﬁciently reproducible results, irrespective of
the application of many combinations of zirconium and
buffer salts, complexing agents and electrolysis regimes.
Apparently, this is associated with the rapid development
of the hydrolysis, which results in the formation of zir-
conium hydroxides and polymeric chains. The latter, on
their part, form polymeric complexes whose stability con-
stant is of the order of 1 ð 10.53 Owing to the bonding of
Zr4C in such stable complexes after a relatively short time,
electrochemical reduction of the zirconium salts becomes
practically impossible. Attempts to increase the cathodic
overpotential have led to the formation of a powdery zir-
conia coverage that has a very weak adhesion to the steel
substrate.
Among the investigated non-aqueous electrolytes of
ZrOCl2 and ZrCl4 salts, the best results have been obtained
after dissolution of the salts in ethyl alcohol. It has
been established that, in contrast to aqueous electrolytes
in which zirconia ﬁlms are deposited at 1.3–2.4 V, in
alcoholic electrolytes good results are obtained within
the range of 9–25 V. The increase in voltage up to
30–35 V deteriorates the ﬁlm quality, i.e. a large num-
ber of pores and cracks appear in the zirconia layers.
For that reason, our investigations on the optimization
of the regime including voltage (i.e. cathode current),
electrolyte temperature, duration of electrolysis, forma-
tion of anode products, etc. have been focused on the
9–25 V range. The current–time plot (Fig. 1) shows
the shape that is typical of layers formed electrochemi-
cally at a high electrical resistance. During a relatively
short time the cathode current visibly drops and reaches
a practically constant value at each voltage. This shape
of curves was a preliminary indication of the formation
of a zirconia (and not metal) coverage on the steel sub-
strate.
Figure 1. Current density vs. time for zirconia ﬁlm formation at
different voltages.
Characterization of the ﬁlms
Regardless of the fact that the current–time curves have
a similar character for the different potentials, the mor-
phology of the deposited ﬁlms exhibits signiﬁcant differ-
ences. This is illustrated in Fig. 2, presenting the SEM
micrographs of zirconia layers deposited under different
conditions. Two apparent changes are noted with increas-
ing potential: the average grain size decreases while the
degree of densiﬁcation increases. A low degree of den-
siﬁcation is exhibited by the ﬁlm formed at 9 V, which
consists of interconnected particles of ¾3 µm [Fig. 2(a)].
At higher cathodic voltages, the densiﬁcation increases,
giving rise to smaller spheroidal particles ¾1 µm in
size, which is most distinct at 25 V [Fig. 2(d)]. The
ﬁlm deposited at 21 V [Fig. 2(c)] shows a very rough
surface structure due to agglomeration of the individual
particles into bigger crystallites. However, at voltages of
30–35 [Figs 2(e) and 2(f)] the ﬁlm shows cracks, proba-
bly due to an increase of the cathode current up to values
at which the predominant reaction is the evolution of
hydrogen.
Additional estimation of the density and uniformity of
the layers has been made on the basis of XPS depth
proﬁles obtained by argon ion sputtering. Figure 3 presents
the alteration of the zirconia coverage as a function of
the sputtering time for specially prepared thinner zirconia
ﬁlms (duration of electrolysis 1–3 min). The Zr/Fe C Zr
intensity ratio is used, which has been obtained on the
basis of the Fe 2p3/2 and Zr 3d peak intensities. This
ratio is <1 when parts of the underlying substrate surface
become visible, giving rise to an Fe 2p3/2 signal in the
photoelectron spectra. Figure 3 shows that ﬁlms obtained
at potentials of 9, 17.5 and 30 V exhibit changes in the
zirconia coverage already at the beginning of sputtering.
This shows that the zirconia ﬁlm obtained at 9 V is very
thin, and the ﬁlms obtained at 17.5 and 30 V, respectively,
are non-uniform. On the contrary, the ﬁlm obtained at
25 V is uniform and thick, due to which changes in the
Zr/Fe C Zr intensity ratio begin after a longer sputtering
time (>200 min). This and the data in Fig. 2 show that
the optimum preparation regime of dense uniform ﬁlms is
that at 25 V.
The XPS spectra of the deposited ﬁlms reveal a binding
energy of the Zr 3d5/2 peak of 183.2 eV and that of the
O 1s peak of 531.2 eV. These values are characteristic of
ZrO2.9–11 Figure 4 shows the element distribution in the
ﬁlm depth. The gradual decrease in oxygen concentration
is associated with the effect of preferential sputtering
during the ion bombardment.12,13 Taking this effect into
account, it follows that the average value of the O/Zr
atomic ratio under steady-state conditions (¾1.65) is in
agreement with the theoretical O/Zr ratio in ZrO2.
The effect of the thermal treatment on the structure and
adhesion of the zirconia layers has also been investigated.
After annealing in air at 550 °C, the ﬁlm remains attached
to the substrate and no cracks and defects are observed,
which indicates good adhesion to the substrate. This
is by the polished cross-section of an annealed layer
(Fig. 5) which shows good adhesion to the substrate. The
growing zirconia layers reproduce the macroroughness of
the stainless steel. On the basis of the cross-sections, the
zirconia ﬁlm thickness is found to be 3–10 µm.
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Surf. Interface Anal. 30, 628–631 (2000)

Surf. Interface Anal. 30, 628–631 (2000)
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
ZIRCONIA THIN FILMS PREPARED BY ELECTROCHEMICAL DEPOSITION
Porous and adherent zirconia ﬁlms with a thickness of
3–10 µm have been deposited on stainless steel. The struc-
ture and chemical composition of the ﬁlms are promising
with respect to their application as catalyst supports in
NOx reduction. Thermal treatment at 550 °C leads to no
change in structure, chemical composition and adhesion
of zirconia ﬁlms on steel substrates.
This work is ﬁnancially supported by the EC through the Environment
& Climate Project ENVA-CT97-0633 and by the National Scientiﬁc
Council of Bulgaria under contract no. X-828.
Figure 5. Scanning electron micrograph of the cross-section of
a zirconia ﬁlm on stainless steel 316 L annealed in air at 550 °C
